mnemonics.title: EOF Detection Methods
mnemonics.content: 'Good E-Fail Bad' - GEB: Good() checks goodbit, E checks eof(), Fail() checks failbit, Bad() checks badbit. Remember: Good includes nothing, E only EOF, Fail covers EOF+errors, Bad covers fatal errors only.

mnemonics.title: Stream States Order
mnemonics.content: 'Good Before Eating Failure Badly' - GEEFB: Goodbit → eofbit → failbit → badbit. This represents progressive failure states in file streams.

mnemonics.title: Read Until EOF Pattern
mnemonics.content: 'Get Line Loop' - GL: Getline returns false at EOF, Get returns stream (true if success), both work for line-by-line reading.

acronyms.expansion: End Of File - the marker indicating no more data to read
acronyms.usage: Used in while conditions, get() return values, and stream state checks

acronyms.expansion: end of file function - returns state of eofbit flag
acronyms.usage: Use while(!infile.eof()) or if(infile.eof()) to check end condition

acronyms.expansion: failure function - returns true for eofbit or failbit
acronyms.usage: Use while(!infile.fail()) as general read-until-failure condition

acronyms.expansion: bad bit function - returns true for fatal I/O errors
acronyms.usage: Use if(infile.bad()) to check for critical errors like disk failure

acronyms.expansion: peek character function - looks at next char without extracting
acronyms.usage: Use infile.peek() != EOF to look ahead before reading

visualTips.visualization: Draw decision diamond: Read → Success? → Yes→Continue, No→Check eofbit set? → Yes→EOF reached, No→Error occurred (failbit/badbit). Each branch leads to different handling.
Show four circles in sequence: Good(goodbit) → eofbit → failbit → badbit. Fill them progressively as errors occur. eofbit and failbit commonly overlap.
Draw timeline: [Data1] [Data2] [Data3] [EOF reached] [Attempt read] [eofbit+failbit set]. Show that flags set AFTER attempted read past end.

analogies.analogy: eof() is like reaching the last page of a book (you know you've finished), while fail() is like trying to read a blank page (something went wrong). You can be at EOF without failing, but once you try to read past it, you fail.

Binary file EOF is like reaching the end of a tape - there's no marker telling you it's over; you simply discover it when you can't play anymore. Text files are like books with page numbers - you can count them.
clear() is like clearing a 'finished' flag on a document so you can continue editing it. The file pointer is still at end, but you've reset the 'we're done' mental flag.

Returning int instead of char for get() is like having a special 'out of stock' signal (-1) distinct from all valid products (0-255). You need to distinguish between 'nothing left' and 'nothing there'.
